translation: When the earth is shaken with its [final] earthquake
commentary:
إِذَا زُلْزِلَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ زِلْزَالَهَا

**When the earth is shaken with its final earthquake.**

The surah opens with a powerful word: زلزلة, which describes a violent, intense disturbance or movement. In Arabic, this term can describe the severe beating of a camel to the point where the animal can barely walk straight, stumbling and unable to maintain its balance. This imagery conveys the overwhelming force of what's being described.

The use of the past tense here indicates a definitive event - this will be *the* earthquake after which there will be no other. While earthquakes have occurred throughout history, this final one will end them all. This is the ultimate shaking at the end of time.

What makes this ayah particularly striking is the phrase  (زِلْزَالَهَا) - "its earthquake." Normally in Arabic, you might say *zulzilat al-ardu zilzālan* to add emphasis, but here Allah ﷻ adds an extra pronoun. This construction suggests that this earthquake has always been waiting inside the earth, ready to emerge. It's as though the earth has been holding this back all along, and it will finally be released.

This connects to another Quranic theme: the earth actually *wants* to shake violently, and the only force restraining it is Allah's ﷻ permission. We read elsewhere in the Quran about corruption appearing on land and sea because of human sins. Judgment Day represents the moment when the earth finally reacts to *all* human sins combined. Natural disasters throughout history have been reactions to some sins, but this will be the earth's response to everything.

Allah ﷻ describes winds in similar terms - like wild horses whose reins have been released. The calm breeze we feel, the solid ground beneath our feet, the stability of our buildings - we think of these as normal. But the Quran teaches us that this stability is actually the unusual state, maintained only by Allah's ﷻ mercy. What we call natural disasters are actually closer to the norm, and our everyday peace is the extraordinary mercy. The time will come when Allah ﷻ decides to let the earth have what it has wanted all along: to shake as it was always meant to shake.

translation: And the earth discharges its burdens
commentary:
وَأَخْرَجَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ أَثْقَالَهَا

**And the earth discharges its burdens.**

Notice that Allah ﷻ repeats the word "earth" rather than using a pronoun. The ayah could have simply said "and it brings out its burdens," but instead says "and the earth brings out its burdens." This repetition serves to emphasize: Do you realize I'm talking about the same earth you're walking on right now? This very ground beneath your feet will give out its loads.

The word أَثْقَالَهَا refers to heavy burdens or cargo - things so weighty they require animals to carry them, not something a person could manage alone. This isn't referring to light loads. The earth has been carrying massive, overwhelming burdens.

The root word *thiql* appears in Arabic to describe the late stages of pregnancy, when a woman is about to give birth and can barely bear the weight anymore. Using this word to describe the earth creates a powerful image: the earth is pregnant with all of us, with the bodies buried within it, with the secrets and deeds that occurred on its surface. Just as a child grows inside the mother, we rest inside the earth after death, and our deeds - witnessed by the ground itself - are buried along with us.

The earth has been holding these secrets, these pregnancies, and it has had enough. It's time to give birth. The ground saw where you went, heard what you said, witnessed what you did. Those secrets died with you and were buried, but now the earth can no longer contain them. This is why Allah ﷻ says in another place: "When the earth is stretched out and casts out all that is within it and becomes empty." It's like a mother finally relieved after delivery, emptied out, free of the overwhelming burden.

*Thiql* also describes when depression or debt has overwhelmed someone - when the weight is too much to bear. The earth itself has been saddened, burdened by what has transpired on its surface. Now it will finally let go of what has been overwhelming it.

When we stand for judgment, we may weigh a certain number of pounds or kilograms, but our deeds? They're like mountains - massive, heavy burdens that the earth has been holding within itself.

translation: And man says, "What is [wrong] with it
commentary:
وَقَالَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مَا لَهَا

**And humanity cries out: "What is wrong with it?"**

After these two catastrophic events - the violent shaking and the earth discharging everything within it - the human response is shock and confusion. People will cry out: "What is happening to the earth?"

There's scholarly discussion about whether *al-insān* (the human being) here refers specifically to disbelievers or to all of humanity. Some scholars argue that believers will recognize this as Judgment Day and won't be confused. However, the stronger interpretation, supported by Imam Bint al-Shati among others, is that this refers to all human beings.

She points to Surah Al-Hajj, where Allah ﷻ says: "O humanity, be conscious of your Lord. Indeed, the zalzalah (earthquake) of the Hour is a tremendous thing. On the Day you see it, every nursing mother will forget her nursling, and every pregnant woman will deliver her load." Allah ﷻ doesn't say this terror is only for disbelievers - He says *everyone*. Every mother will drop what she's carrying. The early events of Judgment Day will be horrifying for all of humanity.

Just as all human beings must taste death - believer and disbeliever alike - there are certain experiences that no one can avoid. This is one of them. It's not a punishment specific to disbelievers; it's simply part of the transition from this life to the next. We will all experience this shock. There won't be secure sections of earth that aren't shaking, with believers safely observing from protected booths. We'll all be rattled. We'll all be in shock.

So humanity will cry out in confusion: What is wrong with the earth?

translation: That Day, it will report its news
commentary:
يَوْمَئِذٍ تُحَدِّثُ أَخْبَارَهَا

**On that Day, it will report its news.**

While all of this is happening - the shaking, the disgorging of the dead, the unleashing of secrets and treasures - there's something even more significant occurring. In the midst of these physical catastrophes, the most shocking event of all: the earth itself will speak.

تُحَدِّث means it will speak, articulate, and elaborate. It will clearly communicate to others about everything that happened on it. The earth will begin testifying about the crimes committed here, the wars fought here, the words spoken here - everything. It witnessed all of it, and now it wants to testify.

The earliest testimony on the Day of Judgment will be the testimony of the earth itself.

This is a sobering thought. Someone about to commit a crime might look around to make sure no one is watching. But who looks down and considers that the earth itself will testify? Every grain of sand, every particle of soil will bear witness.

This extends a theme we find elsewhere in the Quran. Eventually, our own bodies will testify against us. Our limbs will speak, saying, "The One who gave everything the power to speak has given us the power to speak today as well." But here we're speaking specifically about the earth's testimony.

There are narrations that elaborate on this. One states that on the Day of Resurrection, the earth will cry out: "Master, this is what I've been keeping inside." When human beings ask what is wrong with the earth, it will respond: "The way the world used to work is done now. The system of the afterlife has arrived. This is the new order." Nothing is wrong - this is simply the new reality.

Abu Hassan Al-Ash'ari emphasized that this doesn't appear to be figurative language, as if the earthquakes themselves constitute the earth's "speech." Rather, the ayah suggests the earth will be given an actual voice and will explicitly testify. It will be brought to life in a way we didn't know about before.

In a narration found in Tirmidhi, when the Prophet ﷺ recited this ayah, he asked his companions: "Do you know what news it will report?" They responded, "Allah and His Messenger know best." He said: "It will testify about every male and female slave regarding whatever they did on its surface. It will say: 'He did this on that day,' and 'She did this on that day.' This will be its news."

This concept isn't unprecedented in the Quran. Allah ﷻ makes Hell itself speak on Judgment Day. The Fire will say, "Is there any more?" It will call to people trying to flee from it. People will hear its roar and snarl like a lion's growl. Several elements of the unseen will be given speech on Judgment Day, and the earth is clearly among them.

translation: Because your Lord has commanded it
commentary:
بِأَنَّ رَبَّكَ أَوْحَىٰ لَهَا

**Because your Lord will have inspired it.**

All of this testimony from the earth happens because its Master - Allah ﷻ - has inspired it to speak. 

There's a grammatical subtlety here worth noting. Throughout the Quran, when the verb (أَوْحَىٰ - "to inspire" or "to reveal") is used, it consistently comes with the preposition (إِلَىٰ - "to"). For example, "Your Lord inspired *to* the bee." But here, instead of *awḥā ilā*, Allah ﷻ says *awḥā lahā* - using the preposition  (لَ) instead.

This grammatical shift carries two significant implications. First, it combines the meanings of both inspiration and permission. Allah ﷻ didn't just inspire the earth - He also gave it explicit permission to speak. By using this construction, Allah ﷻ compressed multiple meanings into a single expression.

Second, the preposition *ilā* suggests some distance between the one giving instruction and the one receiving it. But *lām* removes that distance and creates direct connection. Notice throughout this surah: there are no angels mentioned as intermediaries, no armies executing commands. It's *Rabbuka* - your Lord - directly inspiring the earth, and the earth itself directly receiving and complying with His command.

There's no gap between Allah's ﷻ instruction and the earth's obedience. The two have been brought intimately close, suggesting the direct presence and arrival of Allah ﷻ Himself on that Day. The direct connection between Allah ﷻ and the earth has been established through the use of *lahā*.

This is the Day when Allah ﷻ will inspire the earth to speak, to testify, to reveal everything it has witnessed.

translation: That Day, the people will depart separated [into categories] to be shown [the result of] their deeds
commentary:
يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصْدُرُ ٱلنَّاسُ أَشْتَاتًا لِّيُرَوْا۟ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ

**On that Day people will proceed in separate groups to be shown their deeds.**

Now that the earth's testimony has begun, the next scene unfolds. The word (يَصْدُرُ) is particularly interesting. *Ṣadr* refers to departing from a watering place in the desert. When travelers journey through the desert searching for water, they eventually find it, replenish themselves, and then move on. The act of leaving that water source and continuing the journey is called *ṣadr*.

Why does Allah ﷻ use this imagery? He's describing our entire worldly life as a brief stop at a watering hole during a much longer journey. Long before you and I came to this earth - since the time of Adam (AS) - our souls have existed. Countless years passed, and then Allah ﷻ took your soul, placed it inside your mother's womb, and gave you a little bit of provision, a little water for a brief time on this earth. This was just a rest stop. Now it's time to move on, because the journey continues to the next station.

*Yaṣdurun-nāsu* - people are done with this temporary stop. The time has come to move forward.

But while at this temporary stop, everyone shared the same planet. They shared neighborhoods, families. Believers and disbelievers lived in the same households. Remember the previous surah's message about how people wouldn't be separated until clear proof came. Now, as people move on from this journey, they won't be together anymore.

(أَشْتَاتًا) - they will be broken up into separate groups, heading in different directions. This picks up a theme from earlier surahs: "Your efforts are diverse." Depending on the kinds of efforts your life was made up of, that's the group you'll belong to, heading in a particular direction based on your actions.

People will be categorized - not by race, not by height or color or language or nationality - but by deeds. The murderer from here and the murderer from Australia and the murderer from another era will be grouped together. The one who prayed regularly, the one who gave charity, the forgiver - wherever they were from, whatever generation they belonged to, whatever society they lived in, whatever prophet they followed - they'll be grouped together by category of deed.

*Li-yuraw a'mālahum* - so they may be shown their deeds. That's one kind of categorization. The Quran mentions other categorizations too: "The Day We will call every people by their leader" - meaning by the prophets they followed or the false gods they worshipped. There may be multiple stages of categorization on Judgment Day, but the central point is clear: people will move on from this life and be separated into groups based on their deeds so they may witness what they've done.

translation: So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it
commentary:
فَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْرًا يَرَهُۥ

**So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it.**

We arrive at the concluding message of this remarkable surah, and notice how the tone shifts. After all the terrifying imagery - the violent shaking, the earth giving birth to its dead, the shocking testimony - Allah ﷻ now turns to something hopeful, something merciful.

(مِثْقَال) means the equivalent weight of something, like a gram or kilogram or pound - a standard by which weight is measured. Allah ﷻ says whoever may have done the worth of a (ذَرَّة).

What is a *dharrah*? If you're sitting by a window on a sunny day and you see tiny particles of dust floating in the beam of light, barely visible - one of those specks is a *dharrah*. These are particles almost impossible to see, let alone weigh or assign value to.

Allah ﷻ is saying that human beings will be shocked on Judgment Day when they're shown good deeds they never even remembered doing. Things they didn't even consider good deeds - Allah ﷻ counted them, recorded them, and now they will see them.

This is the second time in the surah we're told about human shock. The first shock was when the earth was shaking and people cried out, "What is wrong with it?" The second shock is this: when they see that a speck - a flying particle of dust - was counted and valued.

Notice where dust comes from: the earth. In the beginning of the surah, the earth gave up its heavy burdens. Now, by the end, not only are the heavy burdens significant - even a speck is significant. The word *thiql* (heavy weight) was used twice for the earth's loads, and now the same root appears here: *mithqāla dharratin* - the weight of a speck.

It's remarkable mercy from Allah ﷻ that when we think of Judgment Day, we usually think of our bad deeds first. This entire surah has been frightening, overwhelming. But by the end, before mentioning the bad deeds, Allah ﷻ says: None of your good deeds will be wasted. Not a single one will go unrecorded. Even if it's so small it was impossible to measure in this world, it will be counted and measured on that Day.

But here's the crucial question: How can something impossible to measure in this world have value on Judgment Day?

The answer lies in understanding that the scales of the two worlds are completely different. The Prophet ﷺ taught us that actions are judged by intentions. But that's not how this world works - this world runs on results. If an employee did everything right but made no sales, they lose their job regardless of their intentions. If a charity feeds fewer people, its graphs go down, even if the workers sacrificed everything.

The world measures what it can see. The fundraiser recognizes who gave fifty thousand dollars but overlooks the person who gave five dollars - even though that person gave everything they had. In this world, it only added five dollars to the total. But in the next world, those five dollars might outweigh all the thousands donated by the wealthy.

The scales of that Day are different because they're based on the level of sacrifice you made, the level of difficulty you faced. It may be that the fifty thousand was worth nothing, or worth less than the fifty cents given by someone else.

Think about the scholars who transmitted this religion to us. We know their names, but we rarely know much about their mothers, their fathers, the people who influenced them. The love and sacrifice of a mother who raised a great scholar - the world may not even know her name, but that woman is reaping immense rewards from the entire Ummah because of what she did. There are people in the spotlight and people behind the scenes, people who are prominent and people who are invisible.

This is why Allah ﷻ calls this worldly life a game, a play, a deception. Someone looks like a king, someone looks like a beggar. But on Judgment Day, the beggar may look like a king and the king may look like a beggar. Everything will flip.

*Fa-man ya'mal mithqāla dharratin khayran yarah* - whoever does even the weight of an atom of *khayr* (good) will see it. The word (يَعْمَلْ) implies conscious action, something done with intention. Even if it's worth so little - even if our prayer was faulty, our mind wandering, our focus broken - that one percent of sincerity in it, Allah ﷻ will count it. He'll give it to you.

This is why at the end of every prayer, we say *Rabbanā taqabbal minnā* - "Our Lord, accept from us." It's the supplication of Ibrahim (AS) after building the Ka'bah, and how much more valid is it for us! Our worship is broken, flawed, full of problems. But whatever patch job we've managed to offer, we ask Allah ﷻ: please accept it from us.

Notice too that Allah ﷻ mentions *khayr* (good) first, before mentioning evil in the next ayah. This is deliberate and merciful. Judgment Day is terrifying, but Allah ﷻ wants you to see your good deeds first. When you go to court or present your case, if you start with a mistake, the entire impression suffers. But if you start well and then have some errors, the overall impression is better. Allah ﷻ wants to boost your confidence from the beginning: Let's highlight your good deeds first.

translation: And whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it
commentary:
وَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُۥ

**And whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it.**

The parallel structure continues. Just as every speck of good will be seen, every speck of (شَرّ) will also be seen.

*Khayr* doesn't just mean "good" - it means "better," the better choice. *Sharr* doesn't just mean "evil" - it means "worse," the worse choice, and it also carries the meaning of harm. You constantly face choices: Should you say the better thing or the worse thing? Should you do the better action or the worse action? When you chose the harmful option, when you chose what caused damage - *sharr* encompasses harm to yourself and harm to others - that too will be counted.

*Wa-man ya'mal mithqāla dharratin sharran yarah* - whoever did an atom's weight of evil, of harm, will absolutely see it.

Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn't say "will be punished for it" or "will be judged for it." He just says "will see it." The ayah ends with seeing, leaving the outcome open-ended. You'll see what you did. Now what? The surah doesn't answer that question.

That answer comes in the next surah. Once you've seen everything - once you know that every microscopic particle has been counted - then comes the weighing. *Fa-man thaqulat mawāzīnuh* - whoever's scales are heavy. The picture will be completed in the next surah. And once it's weighed, it will be judged: Is it worth Paradise or Hellfire?

But right now, we're left with the shock of simply looking at what we've done. Just looking. The full weight of the realization that nothing was hidden, nothing was lost, nothing escaped Allah's ﷻ record.

---

As we reflect on this surah's structure, we can see it's organized in three parts:

The opening describes the earth unleashing its secrets, and human beings responding in shock. The conclusion mirrors this: human beings are in shock again, but this time not over the entire earth - rather, over every speck of dirt, every particle of the earth, every atom's weight of their deeds.

In the middle of the surah stands the central theme: *Yawma'idhin tuḥaddithu akhbārahā* - the Day when the earth will speak its testimony, when Allah ﷻ will have inspired it, when people will be separated into groups based on their deeds to be shown what they've done.

This brief surah, only eight ayahs, captures the terror, the justice, the precision, and ultimately the mercy of the Day of Judgment. It reminds us that we walk on a witness, we live on a recorder, and one day all secrets will be revealed. But it also promises us that no good will be wasted, no matter how small, no matter how overlooked in this world.

May Allah ﷻ protect us on the Day of Judgment. May He make our good deeds heavy and our bad deeds light and forgiven.
